item.title: Toronto city council votes to ban flying flags of foreign countries at City Hall, civic centres
At historic low point, New Democrats descend on Winnipeg to choose a new leader
item.description: The NDP will have a new leader by Sunday afternoon — and whoever comes out of the party's convention in Winnipeg with the top job will be tasked with bringing the party back from a historic low.

item.title: What's new in Ottawa-Alberta deal to cut methane emissions from oil and gas sector
item.description: As part of the wide-ranging Canada-Alberta memorandum of understanding on energy policy and a new oil pipeline, the two sides announced an agreement-in-principle on new methane rules and targets. It could speed up methane reductions, experts say, but some questions remain.

item.title: Majority in the House of Commons: is the real magic number actually 173 seats?
item.description: OTTAWA - Contrary to popular belief, the choice made by voters in the Montreal-area riding of Terrebonne in the April 13 byelection will have …

item.title: A sign Toronto’s election is unofficially underway? Attack ads target ‘failed mayor’ Olivia Chow
item.description: The ads from A Better City Toronto hammer Mayor Olivia Chow over her record on public safety, affordability and gridlock.

item.title: Toronto Humber Yacht Club shut down by council — even as club members vow ‘it’s not over’
2026 FIFA World Cup will make Toronto even busier. Here’s how the city plans to keep people moving
item.description: To handle the extra people expected during the games, the city plans on traffic restrictions, increased transit service — and more bicycles for rent.

item.title: Star call-out: What’s your favourite thing about spring in Toronto?
George Brown college files ‘mass termination’ notice in face of steep drop in enrolment
Judge calls out prosecutor over allegation she berated Toronto cop about not lying under oath — ‘This is why you don’t go and yell at an officer’
Toronto city council gives green light to develop paid sidewalk shovelling program
item.description: Toronto residents could get paid to shovel their sidewalk as early as next winter.

item.title: Ontario to boost home care funding, may miss long-term care bed goal
item.description: Ontario is investing $1.1 billion more in home health care as it grapples with the reality that it is unlikely to achieve its goal of building 58,000 new long-term care beds by 2028.

item.title: Ontario deficit nearly doubles as province tables $244.2 billion budget in face of ‘unpredictable economic shocks’
Ottawa’s defence spending plan has ambition, but half of the equation is missing
item.description: Mark Carney has told us that all this spending is necessary, but what will Canada stop doing to make way for it?

item.title: Politics Insider: Canada hits NATO military spending target set in 2014
item.description: And, Ontario is projecting a larger-than-expected $13.8-billion deficit for the coming fiscal year

item.title: Liberal MP Michael Ma sorry after confusion over remarks on forced labour in China
10 things to know about Ontario’s budget
Floor-crossing Liberal MP apologizes for behaviour in committee meeting about Chinese EVs
item.description: Liberal MP Michael Ma is facing criticism for an aggressive set of questions at a parliamentary committee where he appeared to downplay forced labour in China.

item.title: Readers share their thoughts about Canada reaching NATO’s military spending targets
item.description: Whether they were glad Canada hit its target or concerned about additional spending, Globe readers had many thoughts on the subject

item.title: Supreme Court justice says decision on challenge of Quebec’s secularism law won’t be based on ‘extreme’ scenarios
Cops at every station. Millions on anti-suicide doors. Inside one councillor’s TTC subway safety pitch — and why critics call it a stunt
Toronto ends practice of raising foreign flags at government buildings
item.description: Council has decided to "eliminate flying flags of foreign nations, or any flags which include the depiction of a foreign nation's flag" — effe…

item.title: Gun charges stayed against Canadian Sikh Khalistani leader
item.description: Inderjeet Singh Gosal, who leads the Sikhs for Justice movement in Canada following the 2023 assassination of Hardeep Singh Nijjar, has had gun possession charges against him stayed in an Ontario court.

item.title: Senate approves budget bill, allowing public-sector buyouts to proceed
item.description: Government’s 600-page bill includes provision for public-servant buyout incentives worth a combined $1.5-billion

item.title: Today at Toronto city council: City to pay residents to shovel snow, orders Humber Yacht Club to close
Watchdog report prompts renewed calls for Indigenous people to run Indigenous procurement
item.description: Federal procurement watchdog found 'cascading failures' in the federal government’s administration of Indigenous procurement programs.

item.title: Ottawa proposes amendments to Elections Act to counter long-ballot protests
item.description: Bill C-25 targets such activism by limiting electors to signing one nomination form per candidate

item.title: Ontario 2026 budget highlights: small business tax cut, AI funding, LCBO revenue declining
item.description: The Ontario government tabled a budget Thursday that focused on developing a resilient economy amid geopolitical uncertainty. Here are the highlights.

item.title: The parliamentary budget officer is caught again in partisan conflict. Should MPs pick their own watchdog?
item.description: It's very possible the Conservatives will forget about their concerns as soon as the new PBO releases a report that is unflattering to the government. But ideally the appointment of an independent officer of Parliament would not be the subject of political conflict and partisan email campaigns.

item.title: Six takeaways from Ontario’s budget, from health care to HST
item.description: Budget predicts higher deficit for the coming fiscal year, gives small businesses a tax break and allocates more funding to hospitals

item.title: Death of First Nation chief's grandson in house fire spurs renewed calls for Ottawa to take action
item.description: The recent death of the three-year-old grandson of Kitchenuhmaykoosib Inninuwug Chief Donny Morris has prompted renewed calls for Ottawa to take action. The Oji-Cree community in northwestern Ontario and a tribal council launched a human rights complaint seven months ago against Indigenous Services Canada, arguing systemic discrimination is behind chronic underfunding of on-reserve fire services.

item.title: Doug Ford’s Tories table record $244B Ontario budget amid hefty deficit
Alberta moves to implement interprovincial pact to ease trade rules on consumer goods
item.description: The agreement doesn't apply to alcohol, cannabis, food, live animals, tobacco or plants, and it lets provinces maintain certain restrictions on items for health and safety reasons.

item.title: Ottawa proposes $1.7B fund to help provinces lower cost of homebuilding
item.description: The federal government is proposing a $1.7-billion fund to help provinces and territories lower the cost of homebuilding.

item.title: Liberal government targets deepfakes, foreign interference in major election law reforms
item.description: The Liberal government is introducing reforms that it says will allow the country to better detect and protect elections from foreign and domestic interference, bribery, disinformation and a host of other potential threats to democracy.

item.title: Ontario budget adds $1.1B for home care
item.description: Ontario is lagging on its promise to build or upgrade 58,000 new long-term care beds by 2028, although due to the growing demand among older a…

item.title: MLA Hon Chan facing ‘serious criminal charges’, say B.C. Conservatives
item.description: ‘Any allegations of domestic violence are taken seriously’ by party, says interim leader Trevor Halford

item.title: Carney government sending $1.7 billion to provinces to boost housing construction
Canada has officially hit 2% NATO spending target
item.description: Prime Minister Mark Carney hailed the milestone as the result of his government's rearmament drive.

item.title: New federal election bill seeks to crack down on deepfakes, long ballots
item.description: 'Canada's elections belong to Canadians. Outside money, influence and threats have no place in them,' MacKinnon said at a news conference on Parliament Hill Thursday afternoon.

item.title: Hospitals get $1.1B infusion from Ford government as critics warn it’s not enough
Toronto’s flawed awarding of snow contracts cost millions more than expected: audit
New Democrats to debate fossil fuel crackdown, electoral reform in Winnipeg
Here’s how Canada hit its NATO defence spending target
Key figure in Phoenix public service pay transition says he’s leaving the team
item.description: The public servant leading the transition away from the federal government's error-prone Phoenix pay system says the team no longer needs him.

item.title: Indigenous services minister questioned about fire that killed toddler
Strategy meant to help Indigenous businesses get federal contracts is 'failing,' says ombud
item.description: A new report says the federal government's delivery of a strategy for hiring Indigenous businesses is a "cascading failure," with inconsistent guidance, a lack of accountability and a lack of proper auditing of businesses.

item.title: Canada could provide vessels, cyber support to clear Strait of Hormuz if there's a ceasefire: minister
item.description: Defence Minister David McGuinty says the federal government is considering sending vessels and cyber expertise to help reopen the Strait of Hormuz — the chokepoint at the centre of a burgeoning global energy crisis — if there is a ceasefire.

item.title: Privacy commissioner challenges Doug Ford’s reasons for changing access-to-information rules
Ottawa proposes election bill that takes aim at deepfakes, long ballots
item.description: Government says Strong and Free Elections Act responds to recommendations from an inquiry into foreign interference and from others

item.title: Carney says apology needed for Indigenous spying program
item.description: Prime Minister Mark Carney says there should be a public apology for a spying operation targeting hundreds of Indigenous people that had the support of the federal government.

item.title: Global Fund disease czar warns of limits to shifting foreign aid to private capital
Ontario to unveil budget against backdrop of trade wars and global conflicts
item.description: It is not expected to have any tax increases

item.title: Ottawa plans $1.7-billion boost in housing transfers to provinces, territories
item.description: Funds can be used to reduce development fees or levies on new home construction, government says

item.title: Toronto school boards want housing development on some land: Star readers discuss
Platform doors on the TTC would save lives and money. Why does it not have them?
Watchdog finds little oversight over multi-billion-dollar Indigenous procurement program
item.description: Procurement watchdog’s probe says Canada can’t 'credibly demonstrate' that Indigenous businesses are benefitting from multi-billion dollar procurement program.

item.title: Ottawa failed to enforce its own Indigenous contracting rules, procurement watchdog finds
item.description: Official criticizes ‘the disingenuousness’ of Ottawa’s goal of awarding 5% of federal contracts to Indigenous businesses

item.title: Canada’s military spending officially hits 2% of GDP, NATO says
item.description: Canada’s defence spending as a proportion of its economic output has not been this high since the end of the Cold War

item.title: Canada clears NATO's 2% bar — after years of lagging and a last-minute lift
item.description: Canada has finally met NATO’s two per cent defence spending target, buoyed by a $9.3-billion surge and internal accounting changes. The long-elusive milestone fulfils Prime Minister Mark Carney's promise under intense allied pressure, but comes as NATO shifts the goalposts farther and scrutiny turns to how the money is actually spent.

item.title: Provinces and Ottawa square off over notwithstanding clause at Supreme Court
Doug Ford and Mark Carney to expand HST rebate to all new home buyers
Veterans Affairs minister announces ‘independent review’ of rehab program that former soldiers say has failed them
Why foreign investment has surged into Canada
item.description: The post Why foreign investment has surged into Canada appeared first on The Logic .

item.title: A Toronto councillor broke ethics rules by calling a constituent a racist, a city watchdog found. Council didn’t agree
Anti-hate bill that provoked bitter debate over religious freedom passes the Commons
item.description: Bill C-9 will now move to the Senate

item.title: Police Federation head wants risk assessment for officers collecting firearms under Ottawa’s buyback scheme
item.description: Brian Sauvé warns that loading of assault-style weapons in cars, en route to hand them in, may prompt 911 calls and tactical units being called in

item.title: Leaderless corporate watchdog remains ‘important,’ Ottawa looking for executive, Anand says
item.description: Canadian Ombudsperson for Responsible Enterprise hasn’t had a permanent head for almost two years

item.title: Contentious anti-hate legislation passes final vote in the House, now moves to Senate
item.description: The Liberal government's politically contentious new anti-hate bill has passed its final hurdle in the House of Commons and will now go to the Senate.

item.title: Quebec’s Bill 21 is being heard before the Supreme Court. Here’s what you need to know
item.description: Quebec’s secularism law is before the court, with debates over religious freedoms and the province’s use of the notwithstanding clause

item.title: RCMP commissioner regrets Indigenous spying program that spanned over a decade
item.description: The RCMP commissioner says he expresses sincere regret for an extensive spying program that targeted hundreds of Indigenous people following a CBC Indigenous investigation.

item.title: Premier Doug Ford, health minister defend supervised consumption site closures
item.description: Ontario Premier Doug Ford and his health minister said Wednesday they will not reverse course on their decision to shutter more supervised drug consumption sites, after a letter from six former Toronto mayors criticized the move to defund the sites.

item.title: National divide emerges over notwithstanding clause at Supreme Court hearings on Bill 21
item.description: Legal arguments at the Supreme Court challenge into Quebec’s secularism law Wednesday focused on how provinces use the notwithstanding clause, exposing a national divide. Some provinces agree with Quebec’s approach while others — and Ottawa — see a need for more limits.

item.title: Doug Ford warns Bonnie Crombie he will ‘send an army’ to stop her becoming Mississauga mayor
item.description: The premier said he would work to help incumbent Carolyn Parrish.

item.title: With hindsight, former immigration minister says he would have capped international students sooner
item.description: Justice Minister Sean Fraser, who was in charge of immigration during some of the years Auditor General Karen Hogan found instances of fraud in Canada's international student program, said with hindsight, he would have acted sooner to fundamentally change it. The Opposition Conservatives have been calling for his resignation, along with his successor and the current immigration minister.

item.title: Ontario health minister 'disappointed' in feds' approach to nurse practitioner rules
item.description: Ontario’s health minister said Wednesday she is 'disappointed' the federal government has left provinces and territories on their own to set rules for publicly funding nurse practitioners instead of creating a consistent, national standard.

item.title: Alberta, Ottawa reach 'agreement-in-principle' on methane emissions
item.description: Alberta will be in control of regulating its methane emissions under an agreement-in-principle with Ottawa.

item.title: Public safety minister calls report of RCMP spying on Indigenous leaders ‘deeply troubling’
Ford government warns school boards to keep graduation ceremonies ‘apolitical’
All Ontario buyers will be eligible for HST rebate on new homes for one year, Ford says
item.description: Premier Doug Ford says the province and federal government plan to temporarily expand the HST rebate for new homes in the province, allowing all buyers a rebate of up to $130,000.

item.title: Air Canada CEO criticized for English-only condolence message after plane crash
item.description: Michael Rousseau is being summoned to testify over the message, which only included two French words: ‘bonjour’ and ‘merci’

item.title: Global Affairs Canada laying off highest-skilled diplomats, union says
item.description: The cuts come as Global Affairs Canada sets out its plans to meet budget belt tightening requirements laid out by Prime Minister Mark Carney last year.

item.title: Air Canada CEO regrets inability to speak French following fatal plane crash
item.description: Premier François Legault called for Air Canada CEO Michael Rousseau to resign, saying he lacks respect for francophone employees and clients.

item.title: Carney 'very disappointed' in Air Canada CEO's English-only condolence video, says it lacked compassion
item.description: Prime Minister Mark Carney had pointed words for the head of Canada's largest airline early Wednesday, saying the Air Canada CEO's unilingual message of condolences to those affected by the recent deadly crash showed a "lack of judgment" and "a lack of compassion."

item.title: Ontarians oppose Ford government freedom of information changes, poll finds
item.description: A poll commissioned by Ontario’s largest public sector union shows widespread disapproval of the Ford government’s recent changes to freedom-of-information (FOI) laws.

item.title: Doug Ford accuses privacy commissioner of politicizing his changes to access to information law
Ontario has finally sent Toronto its share of the money for 2026 FIFA World Cup. Here’s what’s still missing
Ford government planning to waive HST on new homes for 1 year
item.description: Finance Minister Peter Bethlenfalvy is expected to announce that the provincial portion of the harmonized sales tax will be removed for anyone buying a newly constructed home.

item.title: Sponsored international travel by MPs drops to 15-year low outside of COVID-19 pandemic
item.description: Members of Parliament last year went on the fewest sponsored international trips in the last 15 years, excluding the height of the COVID-19 pandemic.

item.title: Toronto school boards fight to allow housing development on part of their land — a move some residents say is ‘outrageous’
Doug Ford says privacy commissioner opposing record restrictions ‘politically driven’
item.description: Ontario Premier Doug Ford said Tuesday he believes the information and privacy commissioner, who is speaking out about changes he is making to freedom-of-information laws, is 'politically driven.'

item.title: Trim OAS for higher income seniors? 73% says yes, new poll suggests
item.description: A new poll finds there is growing support for a proposal to lower an Old Age Security (OAS) threshold for some Canadians in order to help reduce the federal government's deficit.

item.title: Ford says to ‘stay tuned’ on HST changes for home-buyers
item.description: Ontario Premier Doug Ford says his government will unveil details Wednesday about potential changes around HST rules for homebuyers.

item.title: Canada discusses Keystone XL revival with Trump administration officials
item.description: Canadian and American government officials discussed a proposed revival of the canceled Keystone XL oil pipeline during a meeting in Houston, Tx. this week.

item.title: What’s at stake as Supreme Court hears challenge to Quebec’s Bill 21
item.description: From the use of the notwithstanding clause to state secularism to Quebec's unique place within Canada, constitutional law experts say the case will result in a major new precedent.

item.title: Students protest OSAP changes at Queen’s Park
item.description: Students and union members are expected to descend on Queen’s Park today for another large protest over the government’s changes to student assistance.

item.title: Ontario to seize ownership of Toronto Island Airport lands and declare it a special economic zone
item.description: Premier Doug Ford says the provincial government will be seizing ownership of city-owned lands at Toronto’s Billy Bishop Airport and declaring it a special economic zone, invoking new powers that will allow it to override environmental and other regulations.

item.title: MPPs return to Queen’s Park for the first time in 102 days. Here’s what you can expect
item.description: MPPs are returning to the legislature at Queen’s Park this week for the first time since rising for a winter break from the chamber in December. Here’s some of what to expect in the next few weeks.
